popup.title: Alprazolam
popup.content:
Alprazolam
• Brand names: Xanax®, Niravam®
• Class: benzodiazepine (CNS depressant)
• Prescribed for: anxiety

popup.title: Oxycodone
popup.content:
Oxycodone
• Brand names (examples): OxyCodone®, Percolone®, Dazidox®
• Class: opioid
• Prescribed for: pain

popup.title: Carisoprodol
popup.content:
Carisoprodol
• Brand names: Soma®, Soprodal®, Vanadom®
• Class: muscle relaxant
• Prescribed for: muscle stiffness

autopsy.title: AUTOPSY SUMMARY
autopsy.decedent: Decedent: Melissa McCoy, 23-year old white female
autopsy.content: Immediate Cause of Death: Trauma to the chest during automobile accident Manner of Death: Accident Remarks: At the time of death, Melissa McCoy was under the influence of multiple drugs (see toxicology report). Although the drugs were not the immediate cause of death, it can be assumed that toxicity associated with drug interactions was a contributing factor in her automobile accident. Her death can be classified as drug-related.

screen.title: DRUG INTERACTIONS
screen.text: Drug interactions can occur if a person takes TWO OR MORE DRUGS at the same time that shouldn’t be combined.

screen.title: DRUG INTERACTIONS
screen.text: Which drugs shouldn’t be combined depends on the CLASS of drugs to which they belong.
screen.text2: Turn the wheel to familiarize yourself with some common drug classes that can cause drug interactions. Click the wheel to begin.

type.name: cns
type.title: CNS DEPRESSANTS
type.desc: also known as tranquilizers or sedatives
type.example:
• Barbiturates
• examples: Mebaral®, Nembutal®
• Benzodiazepines
• examples: Valium®, Xanax®
• Newer sleep medications
• examples: Ambien®, Lunesta®
• All promote sleep; benzodiazepines also treat anxiety disorders
• Alcohol also belongs to the class of CNS depressants
type.effects: Make you feel calm, sleepy, or drowsy by slowing down normal activity in the brain.
type.mechanism: CNS depressants increase the action of gamma aminobutyric acid (GABA), a neurotransmitter that INHIBITS the activity of brain cells.

type.name: opioids
type.title: OPIOIDS
type.example:
• Natural opioids (found in poppy plant)
• Codeine, morphine, and thebaine
• Synthetic opioids (derived from natural opioids)
• examples: OxyContin®, Vicodin®, Heroine
• Opioids are analgesics (pain relievers), often prescribed after surgery or to relieve severe, chronic pain
• The human body also produces opioids, including endorphins, to relieve pain.
type.effects: Opioid drugs work in the same way as body-produced opioids to relieve pain.
type.mechanism:
• By binding to opioid receptors in the brain and spinal cord, opioids minimize the perception of pain.
• By binding to opioid receptors in the brain’s reward system, opioids can induce feelings of pleasure.

type.name: relaxant
type.title: MUSCLE RELAXANTS
type.example:
• Includes all medications that are used to treat tenderness and pain associated with muscle spasms
• Example: Carisoprodol (Soma®)
• relieves muscle spasms caused by musculoskeletal conditions, such as back pain and neck pain
type.mechanism: Carisoprodol increases the action of gamma aminobutyric acid (GABA), a neurotransmitter that INHIBITS the activity of brain cells.

type.name: stim
type.title: STIMULANTS
type.example:
• Amphetamines
• example: Adderall®
• Methylphenidate
• examples: Ritalin®, Concerta®
• treat ADHD (attention deficit hyperactivity disorder) and narcolepsy (sleep disorder)
type.mechanism:
Stimulants cause neurons to release two neurotransmitters:
• dopamine produces feelings of well-being
• norepinephrine (or noradrenaline) increases blood pressure and heart rate and stimulates the respiratory system

type.name: otc
type.title: OVER-THE-COUNTER (OTC) DRUGS
type.example:
• Group includes all medications that are sold directly without a prescription
• Example: Dextromethorphan (DXM)
• found in various cough and cold medications
• suppresses cough
type.mechanism: DXM (Dextromethorphan) decreases the activity of the medulla, a part of the brain stem that activates coughing.
type.caption: Brain stem

screen.title: DRUG INTERACTIONS
screen.text: Some drugs shouldn’t be combined because they …
screen.caption: … can PROLONG each other’s effects
… can INTENSIFY each other’s effects

screen.title: Turn both wheels to find drug interactions that might have affected Melissa McCoy.
Turn both wheels to find drug interactions that might have affected Melissa McCoy.
screen.instruct: Click and drag Consequence to the table above
screen.warning:
• Memory loss
• “Passing out”
• Slowed breathing (can be fatal)
• Addiction (barbiturates )
• Increased sedation (some stimulants can actually block the metabolism of CNS depressants)

• Extreme sedation
• Slowed breathing (can be fatal)

• Severe respiratory complications (can be fatal)
• Addiction
• Opioids and stimulants usually work in opposite directions, but combined effects in humans have not been studied carefully.
• Muscle relaxants and stimulants usually work in opposite directions, but combined effects in humans have not been studied carefully.

• Extreme sedation
• Slowed breathing (can be fatal)

• Irregular heartbeat
• Dangerously high body temperatures
• Heart attack

• Increased heart rate
• Increased body temperature

• “Dissociative” effects (feelings of being detached from the environment)
• Distorted perceptions and emotions
• Increased heart rate/blood pressure

screen.title: DRUG INTERACTION SUMMARY
screen.respiratoryContent:
• Fatal respiratory distress (breathing stops)
• Severe respiratory complications (can be fatal)
• Slowed breathing (can be fatal)
screen.otherContent:
• Memory Loss
• “Passing out”
• Extreme sedation
• Addiction (Barbiturates, opioids)
